1: The Sermon on the Mount's instructions on almsgiving, prayer, and fasting (ch.6) address the three central pillars of Jewish piety. The critique of doing these things to be seen by men targets a practice that was genuinely common in Greco-Roman and Jewish civic culture: public benefactions by wealthy patrons were carved in stone and announced publicly, creating honor-capital for the donor. Synagogue donors would have their contributions acknowledged; fasting was sometimes externalized through disheveled appearance. Jesus does not reject the practices but strips them of the honor-exchange economy in which they were embedded.
9: The Lord's Prayer (Matt 6:9-13) exists in two versions (Matthew's liturgical version and Luke's shorter catechetical version, Luke 11:2-4) and was likely used as a regular prayer in Matthean communities (the Didache 8:2-3, ca. 100 CE, quotes the Matthean form and instructs prayer three times daily, in contrast to the Jewish practice of Amidah three times daily). The doxology (thine is the kingdom, the power, and the glory, forever, amen) appears in later manuscripts and the Didache — it was added liturgically. The structure reflects Jewish prayer patterns: address, petitions concerning God's honor, petitions for human need, final acknowledgment of dependence.
2:
"Do not sound a trumpet" (mē salpizeis) — probably rhetorical hyperbole rather than a literal description. Trumpets were associated with solemn civic and religious announcements; the image satirizes the fanfare of conspicuous generosity. The stone trumpet-shaped receptacles in the Temple treasury (noted in m. Shekalim) into which coins were deposited made the sound of large donations visible and audible.

"Hypocrites" (hypokritai) — the Greek theatrical term for an actor playing a role behind a mask; in Aramaic the underlying word carried the sense of the profane or impure person maintaining a pious exterior. Apechousin ton misthon autōn ("they have received their reward in full") uses the commercial term found on papyrus receipts to indicate a debt settled — the public honor received is the complete payment; no heavenly account accrues.
3: "Do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing" is a proverbial expression for acting without self-conscious calculation. The right hand performed socially significant acts; the left was associated with lesser or ritually impure functions. Complete dissociation of one hand from the other pictures not physical impossibility but the total absence of self-congratulation — the giver who is not tracking their own generosity.
4: "Your Father who sees in secret" (ho patēr sou ho blepōn en tō kryptō) — God as the discerner of the hidden heart is deep in the OT (1 Sam 16:7; Jer 17:10; Ps 139:1–4). The contrast with theatrical performance is total: the audience of true righteousness is God alone, whose complete knowledge requires no public performance to access. Performing for a human audience is simultaneously pointless and theologically backward — the only Audience who matters has already seen.
5: Standing to pray (estōtes) was the normal Jewish posture (prostration and kneeling were reserved for extreme penitence). Three daily prayer times — morning, afternoon, evening, drawn from Dan 6:10 and formalized in the Amidah prayers — meant that a pious Jew encountered prayer obligations at regular hours. The street corners (goniai tōn plateiōn) at major intersections at the prayer hour provided maximum public visibility for the standing pray-er. The practice need not have been insincere in every case — but the structure of the practice made conspicuousness available as a motive.
6: The "inner room" (tameion) was a storage room or pantry — the least public, most private space in a Palestinian house. First-century Palestinian houses were small, often single-room or two-room structures where family life occurred in the main room; the storage room was the only genuinely private space. The door-shutting (kleis tēn thyran sou) removes even the possibility of accidental observation. The instruction is a spatial enactment of the interior-first orientation that governs the entire chapter.
7: "Babbling" (battalogēsēte) — probably onomatopoeic, evoking empty repetition. The Greco-Roman magical papyri (Papyri Graecae Magicae) are filled with divine names and verbal formulas repeated scores of times to compel the deity to respond — the theory being that volume and persistence forced divine action. This was not limited to pagan practice; some forms of Jewish prayer incantation (especially in the magical tradition of the Cairo Geniza documents) used similar techniques. Jesus contrasts this with prayer based on a Father–child relationship where the child is already known.
8: "Your Father knows what you need before you ask him" — this is not an argument against prayer but against the magical theory of prayer as information-provision or deity-coercion. Prayer in the Jewish tradition was oriented toward alignment with God's will, expression of dependence, and personal communion. The morning service of the Jewish liturgy already expressed this: the prayer formulas did not assume God was ignorant of the need but that articulating dependence was itself the appropriate posture of the creature before the Creator.
10:
"Your kingdom come" (elthetō hē basileia sou) — the Jewish eschatological hope for the decisive inauguration of God's direct rule, displacing all human and demonic powers. The Danielic vision of the kingdom given to the Son of Man (Dan 2:44; 7:14) provides the primary frame. This is not a prayer for gradual social improvement but for the eschatological act of divine sovereignty that would end the present age.

"Your will be done on earth as it is in heaven" — in Jewish cosmology, the angels in heaven do God's will without resistance or delay (Ps 103:20–21); the petition asks for that unimpeded obedience to become the earthly reality. The phrase encodes the eschatological hope for a creation fully aligned with its Creator.
11: "Daily bread" — the Greek epiousios is extremely rare; it appears nowhere in classical Greek literature before the Gospels. Possible meanings include "for the coming day," "for our subsistence," or "supernatural/supersubstantial" (a patristic reading). In an economy where most people lived at or near subsistence level, daily bread was a genuine daily uncertainty. The petition for today's provision echoes the manna wilderness pattern (Exod 16:4): one day's portion gathered each day, deliberately preventing stockpiling, to teach total dependence on divine provision day by day.
12:
"Forgive us our debts" (aphes hēmin ta opheilēmata hēmōn) — the Aramaic word hoba meant both "debt" and "sin," and the prayer reflects this Aramaic linguistic world where sin is conceptualized as indebtedness to God. Luke's parallel (Lk 11:4) uses "sins" (hamartias), showing that the metaphor was understood as moral, not financial. The debt-language also connects to the Jubilee theme in Jewish covenant theology: the periodic release of debts was a covenant mechanism mirroring God's forgiveness.

"As we also have forgiven our debtors" (hōs kai hēmeis aphēkamen) — the aorist perfect indicates completed prior action; the community's prior forgiveness of others is presented as already having occurred. This is the only petition with a human-side parallel, and verses 14–15 isolate it for special emphasis — a signal that this petition carries particular structural weight in the prayer.
13:
"Lead us not into temptation" (mē eisenegkēs hēmas eis peirasmon) — the prayer does not suggest God tempts; the Jewish morning liturgy included a parallel petition: "Do not bring me under the control of sin, transgression, iniquity, or trial" (b.Berakhot 60b). The petition acknowledges human vulnerability and requests that God not permit circumstances (peirasmos = trial, test, temptation) that would exceed the community's capacity to remain faithful.

"Deliver us from the evil one" (rysai hēmas apo tou ponērou) — ponēros can be either abstract ("evil") or personal ("the evil one/Satan"). The doxology ("for yours is the kingdom, the power, and the glory") appears in the Didache (8:2, ca. 100 CE) and later manuscripts; it was added liturgically to round off the prayer for corporate recitation, following the pattern of OT doxologies (1 Chr 29:11).
14: The commentary on the forgiveness petition (vv.14–15) is the only petition from the Lord's Prayer that Jesus singles out for explanation. Its isolation signals its structural centrality. The connection between received and extended forgiveness was recognized in Jewish wisdom (Sir 28:2–5: "Forgive your neighbor's injustice; then when you pray, your own sins will be forgiven"). Jesus makes this reciprocity non-negotiable — not as a commercial transaction but as a character argument: genuine recipients of forgiveness become forgiving.
15: The negative formulation ("your Father will not forgive your trespasses") is the starkest possible statement of the same truth. The logic recurs in the Parable of the Unmerciful Servant (Mt 18:21–35): the servant who received immense forgiveness and refused to extend a fraction of it is shown to have never truly received what was given. The issue is not transaction failure but character failure — forgiveness received without transformation into a forgiving person demonstrates that the gift was not actually taken in.
16: Jewish fasting in the first century: Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement, Lev 23:27–32) was the one Levitically mandated fast. The Pharisaic tradition had developed voluntary fasts on Mondays and Thursdays — the market days when the most people congregated in towns (Lk 18:12; the Didache 8:1 critiques fasting on these days, commanding Wednesdays and Fridays instead). The visible signs of fasting (unwashed faces, disheveled hair, ash on the head) were borrowed from mourning practice (Neh 9:1; Dan 9:3; Joel 2:12). A faster displaying these signs in public was communicating piety through a recognized visual code.
17: "Anoint your head and wash your face" — normal daily grooming. Head anointing with olive oil was standard personal hygiene in the ancient Near East; face-washing was the morning routine. To maintain these normal appearances while fasting makes the fast invisible to human observers. The instruction is not about abstaining from grooming but about letting the fast remain between the practitioner and God, undisclosed to the social audience that would generate honor-capital from it.
18: The "Father who sees in secret" formula (also in 6:4 for almsgiving and 6:6 for prayer) governs all three pillars of Jewish piety. The triad of alms, prayer, and fasting is unified under a single principle: the orientation toward the divine audience rather than the human one. Matthew presents the three examples as variations on one theme, each making the same point with different social-practice content. The repetition is didactic: this is the rule, and it applies everywhere.
19: Wealth storage in the ancient Mediterranean world: there were no banks in the modern sense. Movable wealth existed as garments (a primary form of portable value — Jacob gives Joseph a special robe; Elisha's cloak carries his prophetic authority; the prodigal's father calls for the best robe), as metal coins and vessels, and as agricultural stores. All were vulnerable: garments to moth (sēs); grain and organic goods to rot and vermin (brōsis); buried coin-caches in mud-brick walls to thieves who could dig through (dioryssousin — literally "dig through"). The three vulnerabilities are the actual risks of ancient wealth-storage.
20: The concept of "treasures laid up in heaven" was not invented by Jesus; the rabbinic tradition spoke of this explicitly (b.Pesahim 119a; 4 Ezra 7:77), and Tobit 4:9–10 instructs: "Give alms from your possessions; you will be laying up a good treasure for yourself against the day of necessity." The novelty is making heavenly-treasure orientation not a noble religious practice for the especially devout but the governing principle of all economic existence for the kingdom community.
21: "Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also" (hopou gar estin ho thēsauros sou, ekei estai kai hē kardia sou) — the heart (kardia = Hebrew lev) was the center of will, attention, and fundamental loyalty in Hebrew anthropology, not merely the seat of emotion. The statement is psychological-theological: whatever receives the heart's investment and attention becomes the organizing center of the self. The choice of where to invest is simultaneously a choice about what to become.
22: The "evil eye" (ophthalmos ponēros) and "good eye" (ophthalmos haplous) carried specific cultural meaning in Jewish and Greco-Roman contexts. In Jewish idiom, "evil eye" (ayin hara) was a well-established metaphor for greed and stinginess; "good eye" (ayin tovah) meant generosity (m. Avot 2:9; Prov 22:9 — "a generous eye [LXX: haplous ophthalmos] will be blessed"). The lamp-of-the-body metaphor thus carries a moral-economic meaning: the generous eye (orientation toward giving rather than hoarding) fills the whole person with light; the stingy eye (orientation toward accumulation) fills it with darkness.
23: The escalation from physical sight-metaphor to moral darkness follows the evil-eye tradition's logic: if the generosity-orientation fills the whole person with light, a greed-orientation fills it with complete darkness — and darkness that mistakes itself for light is total blindness without self-diagnostic capacity. The person whose whole economy of desire is disordered toward accumulation has lost the ability to perceive the disorder.
24:
"Mammon" (mamōnas) is an Aramaic loan-word from the root aman (to trust, entrust) — wealth conceived as what one relies upon and trusts in, not merely what one possesses. Personifying it as a master-figure (kyrios) is precise: in the Greco-Roman world a household slave (oiketēs) owed absolute, undivided service to one master; dual ownership was legally impossible (Roman law recognized this). The slavery metaphor communicates the totality of allegiance that either master demands.

"Hate" and "love" in this context are covenant terms for allegiance and commitment rather than emotional states (cf. Deut 7:9–10; the Shema's "love the LORD your God with all your heart"). The Shema established YHWH as the exclusive object of total loyalty (ahavah); Jesus applies the same exclusivity to the God-vs-Mammon choice. Mammon has become a rival covenant lord.
25:
Merimnāō (be anxious) carries the sense of the mind being pulled (merizo = divide) in multiple directions by competing concerns. In an agrarian subsistence economy, anxiety about food and clothing was a lived daily reality for most of the audience, not a psychological abstraction. The teaching addresses genuine material precarity, not affluent worry about comfortable provision.

The qal va-homer (a fortiori) reasoning form that follows — if God provides for lesser creatures, how much more for human beings — was formalized in the Talmudic period (one of Rabbi Ishmael's thirteen hermeneutical rules) but was clearly in use in first-century Jewish argumentation. Jesus uses the standard rabbinic logical form to argue from God's providential care for birds and flowers to his providential care for human beings made in his image.
26: Birds of the air are specifically cited in Job 38:41 and Ps 147:9 as creatures God feeds — and in the Levitical purity code ravens were unclean (Lev 11:15). Luke's parallel (Lk 12:24) specifies ravens, making the point even sharper: even the ritually impure bird receives divine provision. The argument from bird-provision to human provision uses the human's higher status in God's ordering — "Are you not of much more value than they?" — as the premise of the qal va-homer argument.
27: "Add a single cubit to his life" — hēlikia can mean either physical stature or lifespan; the cubit (pēchys) applied metaphorically to time yields "a cubit of time" or "a single hour." The argument is from futility: anxiety accomplishes nothing toward the very thing it worries about. This echoes Ecclesiastes' sustained meditation on the futility of anxious striving (Eccl 2:18–26; 3:9–13): the one who grasps for control and security through effort ultimately secures nothing.
28: The "lilies of the field" (krina tou agrou) were probably the scarlet anemones that blanketed the Galilean hillsides in spring, not the cultivated lily of later Western iconography. Their vivid color and brief life — blooming today, cut and burned as fuel tomorrow — makes the contrast with human anxiety particularly sharp: these exert no effort toward their own provision or beauty and yet surpass in splendor the greatest human achievement.
29: Solomon's glory was proverbial in Jewish tradition (1 Kgs 10:1–29; the Queen of Sheba came from the ends of the earth to witness it). Solomon functioned as the paradigm of God-blessed human prosperity — his wisdom, wealth, and construction projects represented the pinnacle of covenant blessing. To say that a common wildflower surpasses Solomon's splendor in all its magnificence is a deliberately extreme statement about the limitation of humanly achieved glory compared with what God produces without human striving.
30: "You of little faith" (oligopistoi) is a distinctively Matthean category (also 8:26; 14:31; 16:8; 17:20). It describes disciples who have genuine faith but whose trust collapses under pressure or anxiety — not unbelief but insufficient faith. The community Jesus addresses is not accused of having no faith but of having faith that does not yet reach the confidence needed to rest in the Father's provision. Oligopistia is a curable condition addressed by teaching and experience, not a condemnation of non-discipleship.
31: The threefold anxiety-question — "What shall we eat? What shall we drink? What shall we wear?" — echoes the basic material categories of agrarian existence. These were the central preoccupations of households operating without insurance, savings, or reliable agricultural surplus. The repetitive form mirrors the repetitive character of daily worry: the same three questions arising every morning. Jesus does not dismiss the questions as trivial but redirects the orientation from which they are asked.
32: "The Gentiles (ethnikoi) run after all these things" — the contrast is between those who know the Father's provision and those who do not. Gentiles, without the covenant relationship and without knowledge of Israel's God, must secure their own provision through anxious effort — their anxiety is rational given their theological situation. The disciples, knowing the Father, have a ground for trust that the nations lack. This ground makes a different mode of existence possible — not indifference to material need but a different relationship to it.
33:
"Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness" (zēteite de prōton tēn basileian tou theou kai tēn dikaiosynēn autou) — the integrating command for the entire anxiety section and, in many ways, for the Sermon as a whole. The kingdom and its righteousness (dikaiosynē — God's covenant justice, the order of the age to come) are the telos toward which all attention is to be directed; material provision is promised as a consequence of this orientation, not the goal toward which effort is directed.

"All these things will be added to you" (prostetthēsetai hymin) — the divine passive signals God as the agent. The promise is not prosperity theology but basic provision: the material needs that the anxious Gentiles pursue as a primary goal will be given as a by-product of kingdom-first orientation.
34: "Each day has enough trouble of its own" (arketon tē hēmera hē kakia autēs) — kakia here means the practical difficulty inherent in the day's circumstances, not moral evil. The aphoristic closing provides a practical temporal discipline: the horizon of faithful attention is today, not the aggregate of all anticipated future difficulties. This instruction mirrors the manna-pattern (Exod 16): one day's portion gathered one day at a time, teaching that the creature's appropriate scope is the present, and the future belongs to the Provider.
